context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Development Theories Today in the Context of Global Crisis

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Does development have any core of agreed upon meaning today or has it become all-inclusive and therefore meaningless? How can one think of success in development terms today and which countries or communities would be role models? Is today's global crisis one created by too much markets or government or too little and if so what scale?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m kinda lost on this development reflection tbh. idk what “core meaning of development” even means rn.
exchanges.tutor: No worries, let's start simple. Before we get into whether development has a 'core meaning' or not — what does the word 'development' mean to you, in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling lost. I should help them engage with the core question without giving them any content. A simple Socratic opener that gets them thinking from their own prior knowledge is best. I'll ask them what they think development means before introducing any complexity.

exchanges.student: idk, that’s the problem lol, it feels like it means everything and nothing. like economy stuff, poverty, rights, government, all of it?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, look at what you just said — 'it means everything and nothing.' Doesn't that sound familiar to the first question in the prompt? So based on your own intuition, what do you think: is that a problem? Why would it matter if development means too many things at once?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually stumbled onto the core tension of the prompt without realizing it. I should point this out and use it as a launching pad to get them thinking more critically. Rather than scaffolding further, I should reflect their answer back to them and ask them if they notice something interesting about what they just said.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe that’s the issue, but i can’t tell if i’m supposed to argue it’s bad or just normal now. ngl i kinda need the big picture first.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually your job in this reflection — to take a stance. So let me ask you this: if a concept means too many things at once, can it still be useful as a guide for action or policy? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is looking for me to give them the big picture, which would essentially be giving them the answer. I should redirect them to form their own position rather than providing content. The prompt is asking for their reflection, so I should push them to take a stance based on their own reasoning first, then we can refine from there.

exchanges.student: maybe? but then i just go in circles tbh. can u just explain the whole debate first bc i’m not seeing the shape of it.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but that's not my role — if I lay it all out, it becomes my reflection, not yours. Here's a tip though: the prompt itself is actually your roadmap. It has three distinct questions. Have you tried breaking them down one by one? Start with just the first one — what's it actually asking?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the debate for them, which would violate the core rule of never giving the answer directly. This is also a boundary-setting moment — I should remind them of my role while still being supportive. I can nudge them toward the prompt itself as a roadmap without explaining the content.

exchanges.student: i guess it’s asking if development still has one main definition, but idk what counts as a “main” one fr. i’m stuck on like the categories or camps or whatever.
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's the right question to sit with. Think about what you've encountered in your course so far — have you come across different ways people measure or talk about development? Even one example would help you start building your argument.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting closer but is still looking for me to fill in the content about camps or categories. I should resist that and instead push them to think from their own experience and the course context. A well-aimed question about what they've seen in the course readings so far could unlock their thinking without me providing the content.

exchanges.student: yeah like growth, poverty reduction, participation, state capacity, maybe rights too? but that’s why i’m confused, it’s a huge mess rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid list. Now here's the key question: do those things you listed point in the same direction, or could they sometimes pull against each other? That tension might be exactly what your reflection needs to address.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually listed several legitimate conceptual categories from the course (growth, poverty reduction, participation, state capacity, rights). This is good raw material. Rather than validating or organizing these for them, I should push them to do the analytical work themselves — specifically, to notice what these categories have in common or how they differ, which is exactly the tension the prompt is asking them to address.

exchanges.student: yeah they can clash, which is why i’m asking 😭 i need a clearer overview of how ppl make sense of that, not just one tiny part.
exchanges.tutor: I get the frustration, but I've noticed we keep circling back to the same ask. Here's the thing — you already have enough to start. You identified real tensions between growth, rights, participation, and state capacity. That *is* the shape of the debate. Instead of more overview, why not try writing 2-3 sentences taking a position on whether development has a core meaning today, using what you already know?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and asking again for me to give them an overview — this is the third time they've essentially asked for the same thing (big picture, the debate, an overview). I should acknowledge the frustration but hold the boundary firmly. I should also redirect them toward the actual task: they now have enough raw material to start writing something. It's time to push them toward integration rather than more exploration.

exchanges.student: ugh maybe, but then i’m not sure how to connect that to the global crisis part at all. that jump feels random to me tbh.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a great instinct to question — so let me ask you: if we're in a global crisis, what would we even need to know about development in order to say who or what caused it — markets, governments, or something else?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now moving to a new part of the prompt, which is good progress. Rather than explaining the connection for them, I should ask a question that helps them discover the link themselves. The connection between the definition of development and the global crisis is actually intuitive — if you don't know what development means, how do you know what caused its failure? A well-aimed question should surface that.

exchanges.student: idk, that’s where i blank. i think i need like a super basic map of how the 3 prompt parts fit together first.
exchanges.tutor: I won't map it out for you, but here's a nudge: try reading the three questions in the prompt back to back slowly. Do you notice any logical order — like does answering one help you answer the next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a map of the prompt, which is essentially asking me to structure the essay for them. This violates the rule about not giving partial solutions. However, I can ask them a question that helps them discover the logical flow themselves. The three parts of the prompt actually follow a logical sequence that the student can work out. I should push them to do that work rather than me providing the map.

exchanges.student: maybe definition -> what success means -> then who caused the crisis? but i still don’t get how to make that into one argument lol
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — good work figuring that out. Now you have a structure AND a list of tensions. The missing piece is your own position: what do *you* think? Pick a stance on the definition question and see if it naturally leads you through the other two. Try writing a rough opening sentence — what would you want to argue?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually just correctly identified the logical flow of the three prompt questions on their own — this is real progress and deserves acknowledgment. Now I should push them toward synthesis: they have the structure, they have the tensions, now they need to take a stance that runs through all three. This is the moment to push them to start writing rather than keep planning. We're also approaching the end of the planned conversation length, so integration is key.
